# Element Fusion

> A free, browser-based chemistry discovery sandbox for middle and high school classrooms.

Element Fusion is an open-source, single-page web application where students drag elements onto a canvas and combine them to discover real chemical compounds and nuclear fusion reactions. Every discovery includes the actual chemistry: what was made, why it works, and where it shows up in the real world. The game covers two domains: bench chemistry (compounds, acids, salts, oxides, organics, alloys) and stellar nucleosynthesis (the proton-proton chain, triple-alpha process, and the alpha ladder from carbon to iron).

## Key facts

- **Free**: no paid tiers, no locked content, no accounts required
- **Private**: no data collection, no analytics, no server — progress saves only to the student's browser via localStorage
- **No install**: runs in any modern desktop browser; open the URL and play
- **Open source**: MIT license, source at https://github.com/johnathanmoore9066/chem-game
- **Extensible**: all chemistry lives in a single file (data.js); teachers can submit new reactions via email or pull request
- **Audience**: middle school, high school, and introductory undergraduate chemistry and physics

## What it teaches

- Chemical compound formation (ionic, covalent, organic, alloys, acids, bases, salts)
- Why certain elements don't react (noble gases, unstable isotopes)
- Stellar nucleosynthesis: the proton-proton chain, the triple-alpha process, carbon/oxygen/silicon burning, iron as the end of fusion
- Real-world applications for every discovered compound

## How to use it

Open https://elementfusion.org/ in a browser. Drag element tiles from the inventory onto the reaction canvas, then drag one node into another to attempt a reaction. The glowing circle (Stellar Core) is the only place where fusion reactions work — the same two elements behave differently inside vs. outside it.

## For teachers

- No accounts or IT approval required
- Safe for all ages (no data leaves the device)
- Reactions can be added to match your curriculum: fill out a template and email johnathanmoore@elementfusion.org or submit a GitHub pull request
- Submission template and contributor guide: https://github.com/johnathanmoore9066/chem-game#add-your-own-chemistry

## Contact

- Email: johnathanmoore@elementfusion.org
- GitHub: https://github.com/johnathanmoore9066/chem-game
- Support (optional): https://ko-fi.com/johnathanmoore9066
